jokes.text: I love a restaurant run by women 'cause they don't rape or molest their employees, and the food just tastes better.
jokes.setup: Ilana is talking about a female chef's restaurant she's excited about, framed as a food recommendation.
jokes.punchline: The reason she loves it pivots from culinary praise to a darkly comic indictment of male chefs/restaurateurs in the #MeToo era, then nonchalantly adds 'and the food just tastes better' as if it's the afterthought.
jokes.explanation: This works because it's perfectly calibrated to Ilana's brand of confident feminist social commentary — she's not making a joke ABOUT assault, she's using it as an obvious baseline qualifier that reveals something true about the restaurant industry. The 'and the food just tastes better' tag is perfectly Ilana: banal and sincere after the bombshell. High character integration because this read sounds exactly like her and nobody else on TV. Justifies the 8 craft.

jokes.text: Also, I told them already that you don't eat beef. / Why? / So there's no beef on your plate?
jokes.setup: Ilana casually mentions she already told the restaurant Abbi's dietary preferences — for a dinner Abbi hasn't agreed to attend.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's confused 'Why?' reveals Ilana assumed she was coming; Ilana's response 'So there's no beef on your plate?' is smugly literal, refusing to acknowledge the implied presumption.
jokes.explanation: A tight little beat that reveals Ilana's presumptuous best-friend energy. The literal answer to Abbi's existential question is funny because it completely sidesteps accountability. Good character work, efficient.

jokes.text: Don't be a bitch. Who's gonna mediate?
jokes.setup: Abbi says she's not coming to Ilana's anniversary dinner.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response — calling her a bitch and asking who will 'mediate' her anniversary — implies that her romantic dinner requires a third-party arbitrator, which is both absurd and totally consistent with her relationship dynamic.
jokes.explanation: The joke plants the episode's B-plot premise while being funny on its own. 'Who's gonna mediate?' is an absurd thing to need for an anniversary dinner, but it's revealed to be completely accurate. Short, punchy, character-specific.

jokes.text: You Sade-Baderinwa believe I do!
jokes.setup: Abbi asks if Ilana has her 'deal points' prepared for the relationship renegotiation.
jokes.punchline: Instead of 'You better believe I do,' Ilana substitutes in Sade Baderinwa — a New York news anchor — as a mash-up oath/exclamation, a signature Ilana verbal tic of replacing idioms with Black celebrity names.
jokes.explanation: Broad City's recurring bit where Ilana uses Black women's names as intensifiers/exclamations (Raven, Oprah, etc.) is one of the show's most distinctively weird verbal comedy signatures. 'Sade-Baderinwa' as a replacement for 'you better believe' is maximally specific — a New York local news anchor as oath. Extremely high character integration; nobody else on television does this. Justifies the 8+ craft score.

jokes.text: You took the lemons of being fired from Anthropologie and turned them into gorgeous, artistic, bad-bitch-gettin'-shit-done lemonade.
jokes.setup: Ilana is complimenting Abbi on having her art cards in the store.
jokes.punchline: The callback to Abbi being fired from Anthropologie as the inciting 'lemons,' combined with Ilana's sprawling, hyphenated lemonade description, is a characteristically Ilana-brand motivational speech. The joke is how much she's stuffed into a 'lemonade' metaphor.
jokes.explanation: The bit works because 'bad-bitch-gettin'-shit-done' as an adjective string is pure Ilana voice, and the callback to the Anthropologie firing grounds it in show history. Slightly bloated but charming.

jokes.text: Smelly Pussy Donna?
jokes.setup: Abbi is warmly greeting her college friend Donna. The audience expects a normal introduction.
jokes.punchline: Ilana blurts out 'Smelly Pussy Donna?' — apparently a college nickname she learned secondhand from Abbi — turning a normal reunion into an immediate social catastrophe.
jokes.explanation: The delivery is everything here. Ilana saying a deeply inappropriate nickname out loud as a genuine question — like she's confirming an identity — is both perfectly character-driven and structurally clean. The setup (warm greeting) and the punchline (mortifying blurt) are maximally contrasted. The name itself is memorably crude. The extended apology that follows is its own beat. Strong 7.8 craft.

jokes.text: Ilana's extended, escalating apology — 'I am s... I am so sorry. I'm truly sorry. That is so rude. I am... I am so sorry. Clearly that got relayed from me.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just said 'Smelly Pussy Donna' directly to Donna's face.
jokes.punchline: The apology keeps going and going, and then undermines itself with 'Clearly that got relayed from me' — admitting Abbi told her the nickname while somehow making it worse.
jokes.explanation: The escalating apology that implicates Abbi ('Clearly that got relayed from me') is a good extension of the previous joke. Ilana's self-awareness in the apology — knowing exactly what she did wrong — while still making it worse is true to her character.

jokes.text: It's okay. I officially own that nickname, because I beat my smelly pussy. It only took probiotics and six years of therapy, but I'm officially free of malodorous pudenda.
jokes.setup: Donna responds to the humiliating nickname reveal. The expectation is she'll be offended.
jokes.punchline: She has not only made peace with it — she has a formal, clinical language for it ('malodorous pudenda'), complete with a treatment protocol. The pivot from 'smelly pussy' to 'malodorous pudenda' is the verbal escalation that lands the joke.
jokes.explanation: This is a great comedic reversal. The show sets up Ilana's faux pas as the joke, and then Donna completely owns the room by reclaiming it with clinical precision ('malodorous pudenda') and a wellness journey. The specificity of 'probiotics AND six years of therapy' adds escalating absurdist detail. The formal language landing after the crude setup is structurally excellent. Near the 8 anchor.

jokes.text: I just gave a TAD Talk. / You gave a TED Talk about it? / No, TADTalk. / Cool.
jokes.setup: Donna mentions she's used her experience to inspire other women, implying she gave a prestigious TED Talk.
jokes.punchline: It's a 'TAD Talk' — an obvious spoof/lesser version of TED. Ilana's 'Cool' lands as a completely flat non-reaction, implying she has no idea what either thing is, which undercuts any expected enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: The triple-beat structure (setup, clarification, Ilana's 'Cool' as if they're equivalent) is tight. 'TAD Talk' is a clean wordplay joke. Ilana's deadpan 'Cool' is the real punchline — treating both as equally unimpressive.

jokes.text: She's not trying. She is succeeding.
jokes.setup: Abbi self-deprecates about her art, saying she's 'trying.'
jokes.punchline: Ilana's flat correction — refusing to allow Abbi's diminishment — is both a character beat and a small joke. The zero-tolerance for modesty is funny and warm.
jokes.explanation: Efficient and character-true. The joke is in Ilana's absolute refusal to let Abbi undercut herself, delivered with the tone of a factual correction. More sweet than hilarious, but it works.

jokes.text: I have this dinner I have to go to. / That sucks we can't go.
jokes.setup: Donna invites both of them to the MoMA party. Ilana instantly declines on behalf of both of them because she has a dinner.
jokes.punchline: 'That sucks we can't go' — Ilana unilaterally drafting Abbi into her own schedule refusal, using 'we' without checking with Abbi, who clearly wants to go.
jokes.explanation: Classic Ilana move — the codependent 'we' that subsumes Abbi's autonomy without a second thought. The joke sets up the episode's A-plot (Abbi going alone) while being funny in its own right. The beat of Abbi gently correcting it is the structural payoff.

jokes.text: My Pediphile is here.
jokes.setup: Normal conversation about getting around NYC. Donna says 'My Pediphile is here' as if saying her Uber arrived.
jokes.punchline: The word sounds like 'pedophile' — Donna has an app for pedicab obsessives called 'Pediphile.' The misdirection is brief but sharp.
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection — the audience hears 'pedophile' and the immediate pivot to a pedicab app is a nice subversion. The name is deliberately terrible in a way that's funny. The follow-up explanation (she needed to air out her vagina in college — because of the smelly pussy thing) stacks another callback onto it efficiently.

jokes.text: I became obsessed with pedicabs in college when I needed to air out my vagina, because... the smelly pussy thing.
jokes.setup: Donna is explaining why she got into pedicabs.
jokes.punchline: The reason loops back to the smelly pussy condition — an efficient callback that also deepens the Donna character by showing the condition had real logistical impact on her life.
jokes.explanation: The callback lands because it's unexpected — of course her pedicab obsession has a vaginal origin. The 'because... the smelly pussy thing' trailing off as if it's understood is a nice economy beat.

jokes.text: I wouldn't not air my pussy out in a pedicab. / Yeah. Me, neither.
jokes.setup: Donna has left in her pedicab. The girls watch her go.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's earnest statement — a triple-negative that comes out as endorsing pedicab vaginal airing — and Abbi's agreement, plays as sincere when it's absurd.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the earnest agreement — like they've both genuinely considered pedicab vaginal ventilation and found it appealing. Light but charming button on the Donna sequence.

jokes.text: Abbi trails off trying to think of another friend to bring to MoMA — three false starts — then decides it's cool to go alone.
jokes.setup: Abbi says she might ask another friend to come to MoMA with her.
jokes.punchline: She visibly cannot think of anyone — 'I could ask the... No, I...' — which implies she either has no other close friends or all her options are terrible. The comedy is in the pause and self-correction before settling on 'it's cool to go alone.'
jokes.explanation: A relatable character beat played for mild comedy. The implication that Ilana is Abbi's entire social world is gently funny and true to the show's friendship-as-codependency theme.

jokes.text: It is cool, 'cause then I don't have to explain all the art-world ins and outs to someone. Like, it's a lot.
jokes.setup: Abbi is convincing herself it's fine to go to the MoMA party alone.
jokes.punchline: Her justification — that she'd have to explain 'the art-world ins and outs' — is funny because Abbi is not actually an art-world insider. She's a caterer who makes greeting cards. The self-delusion is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A subtle character joke — Abbi's imposter syndrome coexists with occasional grandiosity about her art-world standing. The 'it's a lot' is the self-serious tag that makes it land.

jokes.text: You know what? I'm gonna call Kevin. / Perfect. I'm gonna pet this dog.
jokes.setup: Abbi announces she needs to call her catering boss to cancel her shift.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response — 'Perfect. I'm gonna pet this dog' — is delivered with equal weight and seriousness, as if petting a dog is an equally important task that she has been waiting to address.
jokes.explanation: The parallel phrasing ('I'm gonna call Kevin' / 'I'm gonna pet this dog') as if both are of equal gravitas is a small, very Ilana joke. Pure economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Kevin's reaction to Abbi asking to cancel her shift: 'Fuck! Tonight is already gonna be really intense... But I guess we'll just have to make do without you.' / 'Yes! Okay. Thank you so much, Kevin.'
jokes.setup: Abbi nervously asks her boss Kevin to let her skip her catering shift.
jokes.punchline: Kevin performs inconvenienced martyrdom ('make do without you') but clearly isn't actually going to stop her; Abbi's 'Yes! Okay. Thank you so much!' ignores the passive-aggression entirely. The comedy is in both of them playing their roles perfectly.
jokes.explanation: A functional workplace comedy beat. Kevin's passive-aggressive reluctant approval is recognizable, and Abbi's willful cheerfulness ignores it cleanly. Nothing exceptional but well-executed.

jokes.text: Hot and heavy over here. Does Lincoln know? / Well, I did have a polyamory clause in my deal tonight, but now I have to add a bestiality clause.
jokes.setup: Ilana is passionately petting the dog while Abbi takes her call. Ilana makes it weird.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's comment prompts Ilana to reveal she has a formal 'polyamory clause' in tonight's dinner negotiations — and now the dog has triggered a 'bestiality clause' amendment. Treating relationship negotiations as a legal contract with clauses is the running joke.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the negotiation metaphor — from 'mediate' to formal 'clauses' — is building nicely. Adding a bestiality clause because of dog-petting is an absurdist extension that still tracks with Ilana's logic. High character integration; this is Ilana's specific relationship language.

jokes.text: I thought it was funny. / Thank you.
jokes.setup: Ilana has made the bestiality clause joke.
jokes.punchline: She seeks validation from Abbi — 'I thought it was funny' — and Abbi's 'Thank you' confirms it was, while the meta-awareness of needing the laugh to land is briefly fourth-wall-adjacent.
jokes.explanation: A small improv-inflected button — feels genuine rather than scripted, which is part of Broad City's texture. Mildly funny, more of a grace note.

jokes.text: Happy anniversary/renegotiation/check-in.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Lincoln sit down for their anniversary dinner.
jokes.punchline: The formal three-part toast — 'anniversary/renegotiation/check-in' — reduces romance to bureaucracy. The slashmarks are audible in Glazer's delivery.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly establishes the tone of the Lincoln/Ilana dinner. The bureaucratic framing of romance is very specifically Ilana, and the slash-stacking is a good verbal comedy technique.

jokes.text: The Experience Coupon Book reveal — 'Handjob.' / 'Whoop!' / 'Dry'? / 'I prefer wet.' / 'Picnic in the park.' / 'Blowjob, wet.' / 'Zoo day.' / 'Anal!'
jokes.setup: Ilana gives Lincoln a handmade coupon book as an anniversary gift. Lincoln opens it and reads the coupons aloud.
jokes.punchline: The coupons alternate between sweet/normal couple activities and explicit sexual acts, read with equal enthusiasm. The escalation ('Handjob... Picnic in the park... Blowjob, wet... Zoo day... Anal!') treats the mundane and the explicit as equivalent. Lincoln's 'Whoop!' at 'handjob' and his corrections ('I prefer wet') make it a dialogue.
jokes.explanation: The structure here is excellent — the A/B alternation of wholesome and explicit coupons, delivered at the same emotional register, is a reliable comedy escalation engine. Lincoln's negotiation of 'Dry'? → 'I prefer wet' is a micro-joke within the sequence. 'Anal!' as the final escalation cap is well-paced. The sequence is visually funny too (the book itself). Strong 7.8 craft.

jokes.text: This is awesome. This is a quantified, organized list of the stuff we already do.
jokes.setup: Lincoln is moved by the coupon book gift.
jokes.punchline: His sincere appreciation lands on 'it's a quantified, organized list of the stuff we already do' — which is both sweet and a deflation of the romantic gesture. He loves it precisely because it's efficient rather than imaginative.
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's genuine delight at organizational efficiency as romance is a good character beat. His appreciation is real even as it undercuts the gesture — that tension is funny.

jokes.text: Lincoln's gift reveal — Ilana opens it, farts, it goes up her dress and into her nose, then dissipates — 'Okay. "Jewess"? Yes! / I saw them, and I thought of you. / Why? / Just kidding.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln has a gift for Ilana. She opens it.
jokes.punchline: A two-part sequence: (1) Ilana farts while opening the gift — the fart travels up her dress and into her nose — which is inferred physical comedy. (2) The gift is earrings that say 'Jewess,' which Lincoln got because they 'reminded him of her.' His 'Why? Just kidding.' is the button — he knows exactly why.
jokes.explanation: The fart-up-the-dress is great physical comedy inferred from context — it's Broad City's signature bodily humor. But the real joke is the 'Jewess' earrings: Lincoln buying Ilana earrings that celebrate her Jewish identity is a sweet, specific, character-grounded gift. 'Why? Just kidding.' is perfect — he knows why and it doesn't need explanation. The fart and the tenderness in one beat is very Broad City.

jokes.text: Lincoln's gift reveal continues — cards featuring: Art. / Asymmetrical hairstyles. / Unabashed confidence.
jokes.setup: The gift continues — more items that apparently 'reminded Lincoln of Ilana.'
jokes.punchline: The items — Art, Asymmetrical hairstyles, Unabashed confidence — are not objects but categories, inferred to be cards or prints. Each one is an accurate, affectionate Ilana character trait. The comedy is in Lincoln's earnest specificity.
jokes.explanation: A warm, character-driven funny beat. Lincoln has catalogued Ilana's personality into gift items. 'Unabashed confidence' as something you can receive as a gift is the absurdist high point of the list.

jokes.text: Helicopter! / Abbi! / Kevin!
jokes.setup: Ilana and Lincoln are having their private anniversary moment. Something interrupts.
jokes.punchline: 'Helicopter!' as an exclamation — Lincoln spots a helicopter — is immediately followed by 'Abbi!' and 'Kevin!' suggesting Abbi has arrived at the MoMA catering gig she just cancelled. The misdirection is that we expect the gift scene to continue.
jokes.explanation: A scene transition gag — 'Helicopter!' as Lincoln's non-sequitur observation is funny as a random exclamation, and the immediate pivot reveals Abbi at MoMA in her catering gear. The irony of her being there anyway is the setup for the next joke sequence.

jokes.text: Kevin: 'Thank God you came after all. I was actually going to fire you for bailing tonight, but here you are, in your bizarrely gorgeous catering ware.'
jokes.setup: Kevin is relieved to see Abbi show up at the catering gig — but she's supposed to be there as a guest, not a caterer.
jokes.punchline: 'Bizarrely gorgeous catering ware' — Kevin's compliment on Abbi's outfit accidentally endorses her as a caterer, not a guest. He doesn't realize she's not there to work.
jokes.explanation: Sets up the identity-confusion plot for the MoMA sequence. 'Bizarrely gorgeous catering ware' is a slight but charming phrase. The dramatic irony (we know she's supposed to be a guest) does the comedy work.

jokes.text: I'm, like, literally, like, getting, like, buttery wet like the... / Yeah. / Like the noodles. / I would raw-dog this pasta even if I knew it had a disease.
jokes.setup: Ilana has taken her first bite of the fancy pasta.
jokes.punchline: Her reaction escalates from 'buttery wet' to the line 'I would raw-dog this pasta even if I knew it had a disease' — applying unprotected-sex logic to a noodle dish. The 'like the noodles' self-clarification after the sexual comparison is the tag.
jokes.explanation: 'I would raw-dog this pasta even if I knew it had a disease' is an excellent line — it applies sexual risk calculus to food in a way that's absurd but completely consistent with Ilana's voice and appetites. The character integration is almost perfect; this could only be Ilana. Highly quotable. The 'like the noodles' self-correction is the perfect structural tag. Near the top tier of jokes in this episode.

jokes.text: I'm a gassy bitch. I eat shtinky food! Whoo!
jokes.setup: After the pasta ecstasy, Ilana celebrates eating garlic and pungent food.
jokes.punchline: The self-description 'I'm a gassy bitch' as a proud identity statement, combined with the exclamation 'I eat shtinky food! Whoo!' — the Yiddish-inflected 'shtinky' is the telling detail — is a character-perfect riff.
jokes.explanation: The 'shtinky' (Yiddish-inflected) is the key — it ties back to Ilana's Jewish identity (just established with the Jewess earrings) while also being a proudly self-described gassy person. 'I'm a gassy bitch' as a positive identity statement is very Broad City.

jokes.text: Garlic rosemary lamb riblets with a mint compote? — Abbi serving canapés to the art party.
jokes.setup: Abbi is now at MoMA in her catering gear, serving food — she was supposed to be a guest.
jokes.punchline: The comedy is the dramatic irony of Abbi catering the party she got invited to as a special guest. Her cheerful professional recitation of the canapés to art-world people is the cringe engine.
jokes.explanation: The setup for the episode's central Abbi cringe sequence. Functional but derives comedy primarily from dramatic irony rather than the joke itself.

jokes.text: Jeffrey Sean Michael Thomas is here. He has nudes of me. Sick stuff. / Let's go play nice.
jokes.setup: Donna and Abbi are navigating the party when Donna spots someone.
jokes.punchline: Donna's three-beat reveal — name, possession of her nudes, 'sick stuff' — is delivered as casual social information ('Let's go play nice') as if having someone with your nude photos at a party is a minor inconvenience to manage with politeness.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'He has nudes of me' to 'Sick stuff' to 'Let's go play nice' is tonally perfect — Donna's breezy social management of a potential nightmare situation is both funny and a character reveal. The economy is very good: three beats, each more alarming, resolved with maximum nonchalance.

jokes.text: Hello! / Ding dong! / Let me get in here. / Let me get in.
jokes.setup: Donna and Abbi approach the man who has Donna's nudes.
jokes.punchline: Donna's social entry — 'Ding dong!' and 'Let me get in here' — is aggressively cheerful and slightly unhinged. 'Ding dong' as a greeting to a man who has your nude photos implies she's announcing herself as an arrival/delivery, which is an inadvertently sexual double meaning.
jokes.explanation: Donna's 'Ding dong!' is weird and funny, and the frenetic 'Let me get in' physical comedy (inferred) sells the cringe. Mild but consistent with Donna's established character.

jokes.text: Abbi immediately offers canapés to the art collector mid-conversation: 'Garlic rosemary lamb riblet with mint compote?' / 'Jesus.' / 'This is a spiritual experience.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to blend into the art crowd as a guest; simultaneously she's holding a catering tray.
jokes.punchline: Abbi breaks character by offering canapés mid-social-interaction, the art collector's 'Jesus' reaction implies theatrical disgust or delight, and then 'This is a spiritual experience' plays the riblets as transcendent — leaving Abbi uncertain whether she did it right.
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash of the riblet interrupting an art-world networking moment is good cringe comedy. The collector's 'spiritual experience' response saves Abbi while also being funny about pretentious food worship.

jokes.text: The roller-coaster sex proposal: 'That's a no for me, dawg. Roller coasters are already scary enough without me having my dick out. Plus, there's pictures, and they project the pictures at the end. Nobody should see that.'
jokes.setup: Ilana wants to have sex on a roller coaster as part of the year's sexual exploration agenda.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's objection escalates from 'scary enough' → 'with my dick out' → 'they take pictures' → 'they project them at the end.' Each new detail is more specific and funnier, culminating in the vision of Lincoln's dick on a roller-coaster photo board.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best single joke from Lincoln. The escalation structure is near-perfect: each new consideration about roller-coaster sex is more specific and logical-within-the-absurdity than the last. 'They project the pictures at the end' is a genuinely inspired detail — it's a real thing, and Lincoln's mind going there is both funny and perfectly characterful (practical, dignified). 'Nobody should see that' as the button has great economy. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor — justifies the 8.2.

jokes.text: I can give you Ferris wheel. / Deal! I like that better, actually. / Up-and-down-vibes.
jokes.setup: Lincoln rejects the roller coaster but offers a counter-proposal.
jokes.punchline: 'I can give you Ferris wheel' as a formal sexual negotiation counter, followed by Ilana's immediate 'Deal! I like that better, actually' and 'Up-and-down-vibes' as her rationale — treating 'up-and-down-vibes' as a coherent sexual preference description.
jokes.explanation: 'Up-and-down-vibes' is excellent — it sounds like a real sexual preference taxonomy that Ilana has genuinely considered. The negotiation-as-contract format continues to pay off. The speed of the deal ('Deal! I like that better, actually') is funny because she concedes too fast, implying the roller coaster was a trial balloon.

jokes.text: I want to watch four rom-coms a year. / I object because they are offensive to women. / That is subjective. / I gotta push back, and I'll give you two.
jokes.setup: Ilana wants to watch rom-coms; Lincoln objects that they're offensive to women.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln — a man — objects to rom-coms on feminist grounds. Ilana — a woman, and committed feminist — disputes this as 'subjective' and negotiates down from four to three. The gender role reversal is the comedy.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy via role reversal. Lincoln's feminist objection to rom-coms and Ilana's dismissal of it as 'subjective' is a good inversion. The negotiation of rom-com viewing terms (down to two, one laughing-with vs. two laughing-at) is a terrific extension of the absurd relationship-as-contract premise.

jokes.text: I'll give you two laughing at them, and one laughing with them. / Deal.
jokes.setup: The rom-com negotiation.
jokes.punchline: Distinguishing between 'laughing at' and 'laughing with' rom-coms as separate contractual terms — with a specific allocation of each — is an absurdist extension of the negotiation premise that treats emotional engagement as a negotiable quantity.
jokes.explanation: The formalization of ironic vs. sincere rom-com watching as separate contractual items is a sharp absurdist extension. Economy is high — it adds a new dimension to the existing joke without bloat.

jokes.text: Can I approach the bench? / Yes, you can. [They kiss]
jokes.setup: After sealing the rom-com deal, Ilana asks to approach the bench.
jokes.punchline: Using courtroom/legal language ('can I approach the bench?') to request a mid-negotiation kiss extends the contract metaphor while also being charming. Lincoln's solemn 'Yes, you can' plays the judge.
jokes.explanation: The legal metaphor payoff — using 'approach the bench' for a kiss — is clean and sweet. The characters fully committing to the bit (Lincoln as judge) elevates it.

jokes.text: Abbi being introduced to Hopper at MoMA: 'Wow. That sounds better than... anything I've done in the past eight years.'
jokes.setup: Hopper describes his career — gallery work, studio time, teaching, a residency program.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's sincere, deflated summary of her own career by comparison — 'anything I've done in the past eight years' — is both self-deprecating and genuinely sad. The comedy comes from the utter lack of spin or defense.
jokes.explanation: A perfect Abbi self-deprecation beat. The 'past eight years' is specific enough to be funny rather than generic — it implies she's been in NYC for eight years with nothing to show. Abbi's flat delivery of self-condemnation is a show signature.

jokes.text: Abbi's salmon canapé description and fake shoelace-tying: 'Salmon and cucumber twist with a dollop of crème fraîche... cured with beetroot.' / 'If I start choking, do not resuscitate. This is how I want to go.' / 'My laces get tangled.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is networking as a guest but is also holding a catering tray. A guest at the party asks for a description of the food, apparently not realizing she's a caterer.
jokes.punchline: The guest's 'If I start choking, do not resuscitate' after the canapé description is darkly funny food enthusiasm. Abbi's cover — 'I'm gonna tie my shoe!' — is transparent, and 'My laces get tangled' as a callback excuse is a cringe escalation.
jokes.explanation: 'If I start choking, do not resuscitate. This is how I want to go.' is a good dark-humor beat about food ecstasy. The shoelace escape attempt adds physical cringe. Functional cringe comedy in the episode's ongoing identity-crisis sequence.

jokes.text: Do you have any coke... / I've always known how to clear out a room.
jokes.setup: Abbi is at a gathering of art-world people, apparently asking someone a question. The transcript cuts abruptly.
jokes.punchline: The cut after 'Do you have any coke...' is a misdirection — the unfinished question (presumably 'zero' or something innocent) reads as asking for cocaine. 'I've always known how to clear out a room' as Abbi's self-aware follow-up implies she has indeed cleared it.
jokes.explanation: The transcript cut makes this joke partially inferred. The misdirection setup works (cocaine implication from a truncated question), and 'I've always known how to clear out a room' as self-deprecating disaster acknowledgment is classic Abbi. Moderate effectiveness.

jokes.text: I would like to make out with five... / ty... 'Fivety'... 50 people. / Ilana. / Two. And I have to know about them.
jokes.setup: Ilana is making her case for a monogamy suspension with an allowance of makeout partners.
jokes.punchline: The stutter/correction from 'five' to 'fivety' (a made-up word) to '50' is a great escalation gag — she's starting with a reasonable number and realizing mid-sentence that her actual desire is ten times larger. 'Fivety' as a genuine mispronunciation of fifty (or as a negotiating tactic) is perfectly in character. Lincoln's immediate counter of 'Two' shows he was ready.
jokes.explanation: 'Fivety' is the episode's most distinctive single comic invention. The escalation from five to fifty via a portmanteau mispronunciation is brilliantly executed — it reveals Ilana's actual ambition mid-sentence, and the invented word 'fivety' is a perfect linguistic accident. Lincoln's immediate 'Two' suggests he's been bracing for this. The sequence is tight, character-perfect, and highly quotable. Justifies 8.2 craft alongside the anchor examples.

jokes.text: Communication is dope.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Lincoln reach a deal on the makeout allowance, with a communication requirement.
jokes.punchline: 'Communication is dope' is Ilana's sincere endorsement of basic relationship health advice, delivered with the same enthusiasm she'd reserve for a drug or a food. The comedy is in the register — treating a CBT therapy staple like a cool new discovery.
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum efficiency. Ilana's flat sincerity about relationship communication being 'dope' is a tiny perfect character beat.

jokes.text: I would like to bring up an issue with the state of Maryland. I think I'd like to move there.
jokes.setup: The negotiation has been playful and absurd. Lincoln announces he has a 'state' issue to raise.
jokes.punchline: The phrasing 'an issue with the state of Maryland' sounds like a legal filing, perfectly consistent with the episode's contract-negotiation metaphor. The reveal — he wants to MOVE there — is a tonal shift from absurdist bits to genuine relationship stakes.
jokes.explanation: The formalist phrasing ('an issue with the state of Maryland') is funny while also being the episode's emotional pivot point. The comedy and the stakes coexist — this is where the episode shifts from fun negotiation bits to real incompatibility.

jokes.text: Schools? So, w-w-what, like, cars drive around them slower, and it's safer to walk? / No. Schools are... for the children that I father to attend.
jokes.setup: Lincoln mentions 'lots of great schools' in Maryland as a selling point.
jokes.punchline: Ilana genuinely doesn't process 'schools' as a reason to settle somewhere — she interprets it through pedestrian safety (school zones) rather than family planning. Lincoln's patient clarification — 'for the children that I father to attend' — is the landing.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest character-comedy moments. Ilana's brain genuinely does not connect 'schools' to 'children' as a motivation for choosing where to live — she interprets it through the lens of someone who has never thought about parenthood. The school-zone safety interpretation is inspired and deeply character-specific. Lincoln's patient, clinical 'for the children that I father to attend' makes the stakes explicit in the most formal possible way. The comedy and the tragedy fuse here — it's both hilarious and genuinely sad that they're so far apart. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Is this about the rom-coms? 'Cause I'll watch one every weekend, you know? And I'll just rip a bong and laugh really sincerely, and I'll learn to love them.
jokes.setup: Lincoln has revealed he wants marriage and children. Ilana panics and tries to find a negotiable fix.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's desperate pivot to rom-coms as the solvable issue — offering to 'rip a bong and laugh really sincerely' at them — misidentifies the scale of the problem while also being a perfectly Ilana solution to the rom-com issue (drug-assisted sincere engagement).
jokes.explanation: 'I'll just rip a bong and laugh really sincerely, and I'll learn to love them' is an excellent line. It's a perfect callback (rom-coms were the lightest negotiation point), a perfect character beat (weed as a solution to emotional authenticity), and it reveals Ilana's avoidance mechanism in the most charming possible way. 'Laugh really sincerely' is particularly good — the effort required to laugh sincerely via drug assistance is the absurdist core. Justifies 8.1 craft.

jokes.text: Lincoln, I'm only 27. What am I, a child bride?
jokes.setup: Lincoln reveals he wants kids and marriage. Ilana registers that she's too young.
jokes.punchline: 'What am I, a child bride?' as a response to being 27 — dramatically overstating her youth. The comedy is in treating 27 as an impossibly young age for marriage while also using 'child bride' as a real self-descriptor.
jokes.explanation: Sharp and economical. 'Child bride' as a label for a 27-year-old being asked about marriage is an absurdist overcorrection that's very Ilana. The line is slightly undercut by the emotional weight of the scene but still lands.

jokes.text: Lincoln, I just figured out that I want to go back to school and focus on my wizness.
jokes.setup: Ilana is making her case for why she's not ready for the future Lincoln envisions.
jokes.punchline: 'My wizness' — presumably 'business' rendered with a W, possibly referencing 'The Wiz' or just Ilana-speak for her professional/creative ambitions. The specific word choice is funny and slightly enigmatic.
jokes.explanation: 'Wizness' as a word is distinctively Ilana — a slightly magical reframing of 'business' that suggests her ambitions are untamed and idiosyncratic. The joke works on pure character-voice level. Slightly muddled in context.

jokes.text: I'm on a New York City timeline.
jokes.setup: Ilana is explaining why she can't commit to Lincoln's suburban family timeline.
jokes.punchline: A 'New York City timeline' — the concept that New York distorts and delays normal adult milestones — is stated as a real, understood social phenomenon that excuses her from standard life expectations. It's both an earnest self-explanation and a gently satirical observation.
jokes.explanation: 'New York City timeline' is a great bit of observed social taxonomy — it's a real thing people say and believe, and Ilana invoking it to justify never having to grow up is both funny and true. Highly economical; four words do a lot of work.

jokes.text: I could commit to a kid when I'm like... 43.
jokes.setup: Ilana allows that she might someday want children.
jokes.punchline: The specific age — 43 — is the joke. It's both biologically late and weirdly specific, suggesting Ilana has actually calculated a timeline but it's one that maximizes delay.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 43 — not 40, not 'someday,' but 43 — is the comedic precision that makes it land. Ilana offering this as a genuine olive branch while it's clearly not workable for Lincoln is a well-executed character beat.

jokes.text: Lincoln's long pause — 'I have no counter.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has laid out her position: 27, NYC timeline, maybe kids at 43.
jokes.punchline: 'I have no counter' — Lincoln, the negotiator, acknowledges that the deal points are incompatible within the established contract framework. Using negotiation language to describe an emotional impasse is the button on the running gag while also being genuinely sad.
jokes.explanation: Four words. 'I have no counter' is the perfect synthesis of the episode's comedic and emotional registers — it uses the relationship-as-contract language that has been the B-plot's spine to deliver a genuine, crushing romantic impasse. The joke is that there IS no joke here anymore; the metaphor has collapsed under real feeling. The precision of the negotiation language making the breakup feel more final, not less, is mature comedy writing. One of the episode's best moments. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Long silence at the restaurant after Lincoln's 'I have no counter' — then: 'Riblets!' / [longer silence] / 'Riblets. Am I right?'
jokes.setup: The Lincoln/Ilana scene has just ended on an emotional cliffhanger. We cut to Abbi at MoMA, passing riblets.
jokes.punchline: The almost-minute-long gap between [13:41] and [14:48] implies a sustained emotional silence at the Dobby dinner, followed by the comedic release of cutting to Abbi shouting 'Riblets!' The 'Riblets. Am I right?' into silence is both her attempting to connect with art-world guests and a tonal reset from the heavy Lincoln scene.
jokes.explanation: The structural comedy here is exceptional. The minute-long silence in the transcript between Lincoln's 'I have no counter' and 'Riblets!' is clearly a sustained, agonizing emotional beat at the dinner, followed by a scene cut to Abbi's oblivious riblet hawking. The contrast — profound romantic grief cut to 'Riblets. Am I right?' — is the joke and it's beautifully constructed. 'Am I right?' into silence is a perfect cringe tag. Justifies the 8.0 craft score.

jokes.text: Abbi trips and spills riblets on a guest at the MoMA party. / Kevin: 'I'm so sorry. We were short caterers, and Abbi's never really been talented with the tray.' / Donna: 'I'm so sorry. Actually, Abbi's an up-and-coming artist and my personal guest, not a caterer.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been navigating both identities — caterer and guest — at the same party. It climaxes in a collision.
jokes.punchline: Kevin and Donna simultaneously 'defend' Abbi with contradictory explanations — Kevin apologizes for her catering incompetence while Donna asserts she's a respected artist/guest — creating maximum identity-crisis comedy.
jokes.explanation: The dual-explanation comedy — Kevin and Donna providing opposite characterizations simultaneously — is well-constructed cringe. Both are trying to help and both are making it worse. The physical comedy of the spill precipitating the identity crisis is the episode's A-plot climax.

jokes.text: So, I-I'm... I-I'm actually catering, but I am... am also a-a guest.
jokes.setup: Kevin and Donna have given contradictory explanations. Abbi tries to resolve it.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's stammered attempt to hold both identities simultaneously — 'I'm actually catering but I am also a guest' — is a cringe comedy beat that also articulates the episode's central tension about her artistic identity vs. her service-industry survival.
jokes.explanation: The stammer and dual-identity claim is the episode's thematic joke made explicit. Abbi can't separate her catering self from her artist self because they literally overlap in this moment. Good cringe comedy with real thematic weight.

jokes.text: Okay, Ab, we're gonna need you to start loading some dirties into a van. / Totally. I will do that.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just tried to assert her dual identity as artist/guest.
jokes.punchline: Kevin ignores all of it and immediately assigns her a menial catering task; Abbi's immediate 'Totally. I will do that' is defeated capitulation — she reverts to service-worker mode within seconds of claiming artistic identity.
jokes.explanation: The comedy of Abbi's instant deflation — from 'I'm an artist' to 'Totally. I will do that' in under ten seconds — is both funny and a bit sad. The efficiency of the beat is very good.

jokes.text: Don't worry about her. She can get her own canapés. / It's not that bad! I don't even like this dress. It's too yellow.
jokes.setup: Donna tries to comfort Abbi after the disaster.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's attempt to minimize the disaster by attacking her own dress — 'It's not that bad! I don't even like this dress. It's too yellow.' — is a comedic non-sequitur defense mechanism. The dress critique has nothing to do with anything.
jokes.explanation: 'It's too yellow' as a pivot from professional humiliation is a funny non-sequitur. Abbi's bargaining-stage response to disaster is well-observed.

jokes.text: I'm just not, like, a... a real one. [referring to being an artist] / Well, you might not want to say that.
jokes.setup: Abbi is lamenting to a stranger (unidentified at this point) that she's not a 'real' artist.
jokes.punchline: The stranger's dry 'Well, you might not want to say that' is advice delivered with specific knowledge that the audience doesn't yet have — setting up the Toy Harris reveal. The comedy is in the dramatic irony (we sense this person is important).
jokes.explanation: The economy of the stranger's advice — not explaining why, just 'you might not want to say that' — is elegant setup for the Toy Harris reveal. The joke works on first watch as gentle irony and on rewatch as knowing foreshadowing.

jokes.text: You're T... You're Toy Harris? / [long pause] / Holy shit, dude. / My God. / You're the third floor. / It took us years of protesting, but we're here.
jokes.setup: The stranger reveals she is Toy Harris — apparently a major, famous artist whose work has a dedicated floor at MoMA.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's escalating reactions — stutter, 'Holy shit,' 'You're the third floor' — culminate in 'It took us years of protesting, but we're here' as a callback to activism getting her work into MoMA. The comedy is Abbi's complete dissolution and the revelation of who she just spilled riblets on.
jokes.explanation: 'You're the third floor' is the line — reducing a major artist to her MoMA real estate is both funny and accidentally affectionate. The reveal-and-reaction sequence is well-paced. The horror of having spilled on her plus the delight of her having given career advice lands cleanly.

jokes.text: You're T... You're Toy Harris? / [beat] / Holy shit, dude. / My God. / You're Toy Harris? / Yeah. / You're the third floor.
jokes.setup: The stranger who was just comforting Abbi reveals she is Toy Harris — a major artist
jokes.punchline: The revelation lands in escalating waves: Abbi's double-take, Donna's confirmation, 'You're the third floor' as the specific shorthand for an entire MoMA floor dedicated to her work — the understatement of the reveal matches the magnitude of it
jokes.explanation: The Toy Harris reveal is a beautifully structured comedy beat. 'You're the third floor' as the measure of artistic greatness is a perfect MoMA-insider line — it's more specific and therefore funnier than any amount of 'you're so famous.' The escalating reaction between Abbi and Donna, the shared disbelief, and Toy's patient 'Yeah' all work together. The payoff on a cold setup (the stranger giving advice) is excellent structural comedy.

jokes.text: I spilled on Toy Harris! / Okay. / I am getting dizzy! / This is insane! / All right, then. Good night.
jokes.setup: Abbi has processed that she spilled on Toy Harris — who has now told her she's an artist and given her career advice.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's manic spiral of self-congratulation and horror while Toy Harris calmly exits with 'All right, then. Good night' is the comedy of celebrity departure. Toy Harris leaves precisely because Abbi is too much.
jokes.explanation: Toy Harris's 'All right, then. Good night' as an exit line — the elegant departure of someone who is done being a plot device — is the sharpest part. Abbi's spiral is well-performed but the real joke is in Toy's economy.

jokes.text: This is not her night. / Bah! Bee! Hee-hee-hoo! / What do they do? Hoo-hoo-hee! / Put your head out of the window now for fresh air!
jokes.setup: After the Toy Harris meltdown, someone (Donna?) is in a pedicab.
jokes.punchline: The pedicab driver's ecstatic/chaotic vocalisations ('Bah! Bee! Hee-hee-hoo!') and instruction to put one's head out of the window for fresh air is an absurdist comedic intrusion — a pedicab driver with a unique philosophy of passenger management.
jokes.explanation: A weird digression that works on pure absurdist energy. The pedicab driver's vocal intervention ('Hee-hee-hoo!') and fresh-air protocol is random but committed. Broad City's willingness to go this weird is part of its charm. Structurally loose but the energy is right.

jokes.text: You keep your head out of the window t... / You keep your head out, too. / Jesus Christ!
jokes.setup: The pedicab driver told a passenger to put their head out for fresh air.
jokes.punchline: The driver is now also putting their own head out of the window — apparently the whole pedicab crew is head-out — and 'Jesus Christ!' from someone (presumably a bystander or passenger) is the reaction to this shared absurdity.
jokes.explanation: An extension of the pedicab absurdism. The driver joining the passenger in head-out-the-window is a small physical escalation. Works as a quick comedic beat.

jokes.text: The ER scene — Abbi has nearly fainted. 'Your girlfriend said you narrowly missed fainting into something dangerous... a sculpture, I think?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is being treated in an ER after fainting at MoMA.
jokes.punchline: 'A sculpture, I think?' — the doctor's casual aside that the dangerous object Abbi almost fell into was a piece of art at MoMA. The 'I think?' uncertainty implies the doctor received this description and still isn't sure what a sculpture is doing in an ER story.
jokes.explanation: A well-placed comedic detail that contextualizes Abbi's night. 'A sculpture, I think?' conveys the absurdity of the evening's climax with perfect economy.

jokes.text: Wait, is Ilana here? / No, she's not my girlfriend. People do talk, though. / No, Donna? / The woman who brought you in. / Yeah, yeah, Smelly Pussy Do... Donna.
jokes.setup: Abbi asks about who accompanied her to the ER.
jokes.punchline: Abbi almost says 'Smelly Pussy Donna' to the ER doctor — catching herself mid-nickname. The 'Smelly Pussy Do... Donna' self-correction, now in a medical context, is a late callback to the opening joke.
jokes.explanation: The callback works because it's the same joke (Abbi almost saying the nickname) but now Abbi is the one catching herself — role reversal from Ilana's original blurt. The ER context makes it funnier. 'People do talk, though' from the doctor is a bonus beat.

jokes.text: Yeah, yeah, Smelly Pussy Do... Donna.
jokes.setup: The doctor says Donna brought her in
jokes.punchline: Abbi almost says 'Smelly Pussy Donna' before catching herself — the nickname having become her default internal reference even after spending the whole episode trying to suppress it
jokes.explanation: A tight callback that pays off the running gag economically. The self-correction mid-nickname shows the nickname has colonized Abbi's mental vocabulary. The 'Do...' trail-off and pivot is the best version of this joke — it acknowledges that Abbi now genuinely thinks of her this way. Good economy in the delivery.

jokes.text: That's... surprising. [re: Abbi not having a girlfriend or boyfriend] / You just seem like you would. / Thanks. / No, you, too. You see... I mean, obviously, you have one... You do. You're a doctor, so of cour... / I don't. / Cool.
jokes.setup: Abbi clarifies she doesn't have a girlfriend or boyfriend. The doctor responds with an attractiveness compliment.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's panicked attempt to reciprocate the compliment — assuming the doctor must have a partner — and the doctor's 'I don't.' / Abbi's 'Cool.' is a classic awkward mutual-flirtation beat, landing with perfect timing.
jokes.explanation: The 'of cour... / I don't. / Cool.' beat is well-executed rom-com cringe — Abbi's assumption that the beautiful doctor must be taken, followed by the revelation and her very flat 'Cool,' is a recognizable but well-crafted flirtation comedy moment.

jokes.text: Fart away, man.
jokes.setup: Abbi and the doctor have established a flirtatious chemistry. Something prompts this.
jokes.punchline: Context suggests the doctor (or Abbi) needs to fart, and 'Fart away, man' is Abbi's way of signaling comfort/intimacy. The casualness of permitting a fart as a flirtation move is peak Broad City — bodily function as romantic gesture.
jokes.explanation: 'Fart away, man' — three words that establish intimacy via bodily permission — is a concise, characterful, highly quotable Broad City line. The 'man' is the perfect casualizing add-on. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: You know, while I was breaking your toilet, I had a lot of time to think...
jokes.setup: The scene cuts to Ilana at Lincoln's/somewhere, and she opens with this.
jokes.punchline: 'Breaking your toilet' as a casual aside — something Ilana did offhand during the dinner that she treats as incidental — is the kind of background Broad City chaos that the show normalizes. The fact that she had 'a lot of time to think' while breaking a toilet suggests an extended toilet-destruction incident.
jokes.explanation: 'While I was breaking your toilet' as a casual setup phrase is excellent character comedy. The show never explains what Ilana did to the toilet — it's funnier unexplained. Delivered mid-sentence as if it requires no comment. High character integration (only Ilana) and good economy.

jokes.text: I'm meant to be a polyamorous queen. / I understand. / You got too much love for just one person.
jokes.setup: Ilana is processing the breakup by reframing it as a personal revelation.
jokes.punchline: Lincoln's 'You got too much love for just one person' — delivered without irony, as a genuine affirmation of her self-concept — is both sweet and funny because it's a completely specific Lincoln response: accepting, generous, a little absurd.
jokes.explanation: Lincoln's sincere endorsement of Ilana's self-description is generous-to-absurdity in a way that's very Lincoln. The joke is that he means it completely, which is both the funniest and saddest thing he could say.

jokes.text: Do you think I'll figure it out like you, by the time I'm 40? / I'm 32.
jokes.setup: Lincoln asks if he'll figure out his future by 40, referencing Ilana's '43' from earlier.
jokes.punchline: Ilana clarifies she's 32 — not 40 — which both corrects Lincoln's math and reveals she thinks she has 11 more years before she needs to adult. Lincoln's question also implies he assumed she was older, which is a subtle age-joke.
jokes.explanation: Clean and economical. 'I'm 32' corrects the assumption while landing as both a laugh and a character reveal (she still has 11 years of 'figuring it out' by her own math). Two words, well-placed.

jokes.text: You know, that coupon book never expires. / Yeah? / So say you have kids, and you have a wife, and you guys are, like, doin' the suburban grind — I will fly in.
jokes.setup: They're processing the end of the relationship. Ilana references the coupon book she gave him.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's offer — to 'fly in' from New York to service the coupon book if Lincoln marries and suburbanizes — is an absurdly specific arrangement that is both generous and completely insane. 'I will fly in' delivered with total sincerity is the dark romantic comedy beating heart of the show.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's emotional and comedic peak. 'I will fly in' — to redeem a handjob coupon for her ex who has married and suburbanized — is a profound, absurdist, character-defining moment. The coupon book callback is perfectly earned, and the specific vision of Lincoln 'doing the suburban grind' before Ilana's commuter-service arrival is brilliant. It's funny because it's exactly what Ilana would do and exactly who she is. High across every craft dimension. The best joke in the episode.

jokes.text: What? / Is it insane that I'm still hungry? / Nope, not at all. / And that's why I brought the cake in here.
jokes.setup: They've just processed their breakup.
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately pivots from profound romantic loss to hunger. Lincoln, having anticipated this, has already brought cake. The joke is Lincoln knowing Ilana well enough to preemptively bring breakup cake.
jokes.explanation: Lincoln preemptively bringing cake because he knows Ilana will be immediately hungry after a breakup is a perfect character comedy moment. It shows his deep love and knowledge of her even as they're breaking up. 'That's a good friend' as the episode's implicit tag here is earned.

jokes.text: That is a good friend.
jokes.setup: Lincoln has brought cake to the breakup.
jokes.punchline: Calling your ex-boyfriend a 'good friend' immediately after breaking up — not 'good boyfriend,' 'good man,' but 'good friend' — is an unintentional reframing. Or entirely intentional Ilana redefinition of the relationship in real time.
jokes.explanation: 'That is a good friend' in the context of a breakup is simultaneously a funny understatement and a characteristically Ilana repositioning — she's already updated his status. The word 'friend' lands as both sincere and a tiny comic devastation.

jokes.text: Okay. So, is that... That's it? / Unless there's something else I can do for you. / N... I-I don't think so.
jokes.setup: The doctor has finished Abbi's medical discharge and there's a clear mutual attraction
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'Is that... That's it?' is transparently an attempt to extend the interaction, and the doctor's 'Unless there's something else I can do for you' is an equally transparent opening — their mutual interest expressed entirely through medical discharge procedure
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy in the will-they-won't-they register. Both characters using discharge procedure language to flirt is a sweet form of awkwardness. Abbi's stammer ('N... I-I don't think so') is physically funny in transcript.

jokes.text: You know, since we're done, why don't you call me Leslie?
jokes.setup: Abbi calls the doctor 'Dr. Marnel' during their extended farewell
jokes.punchline: The doctor's invitation to use her first name — 'since we're done' — is technically about the patient-doctor relationship being concluded, but clearly also an invitation to a personal relationship
jokes.explanation: 'Since we're done' works beautifully as a double meaning: done with the medical relationship (clinical) and 'done' as a prelude to starting something personal. Clean structure with good economy.

jokes.text: Hey, Leslie. / Yes? / I was just thinking, would it be illegal or whatever if I... if I got your phone number? / It would not be illegal.
jokes.setup: Abbi has been discharged but comes back to ask the doctor out.
jokes.punchline: Framing asking for a phone number as a potential illegality — 'would it be illegal or whatever' — is a nervous, slightly absurd formulation. The doctor's 'It would not be illegal' matches the register perfectly, making it a duet of awkward formalism.
jokes.explanation: The legal framing of a phone number request — echoing the episode's negotiation/contract theme — is both character-specific (Abbi's awkward earnestness) and tonally consistent. 'It would not be illegal' is a perfect deadpan response that neither commits nor rejects, letting the tension simmer.

jokes.text: So, even though it was a conscious uncoupling, and we will stay friends, it was, devastating and tragically bittersweet and fully broke my heart.
jokes.setup: Ilana is recounting the breakup to Abbi.
jokes.punchline: Using 'conscious uncoupling' — the celebrity therapy-speak term for Gwyneth Paltrow's divorce — to describe her own breakup is both a pop-culture joke and a perfectly Ilana maneuver of aestheticizing her pain. The pivot from 'we will stay friends' to 'fully broke my heart' in one sentence shows the gap between her framing and her feeling.
jokes.explanation: 'Conscious uncoupling' lands because it's such a specific piece of celeb-speak to apply to a Comedy Central character's breakup. The structure — wellness framing immediately contradicted by 'fully broke my heart' — reveals Ilana's emotional honesty breaking through her performance.

jokes.text: And I'm just praying that your nose returns to its beautiful goyish shape.
jokes.setup: Ilana is summarizing Abbi's night, which ended in an ER visit for nearly fainting into a sculpture.
jokes.punchline: 'Your beautiful goyish shape' — Ilana commenting on Abbi's non-Jewish nose shape as a specific concern following the almost-sculpture-collision. 'Goyish' is the comedic word; Ilana's Jewishness inflecting her concern for Abbi's nose is absurdly specific.
jokes.explanation: 'Your beautiful goyish shape' is a highly specific, deeply character-integrated line. Only Ilana would (a) pray for her friend's nose and (b) specify its ethnic aesthetic category in the prayer. The Jewess earrings callback adds resonance. Near the top of the episode's craft scores. Justifies 8.0.

jokes.text: She's kind of like my... friend. / Totally! Right? / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Abbi describes Smelly Pussy Donna as her 'friend.'
jokes.punchline: She trails off before 'friend' — she's slightly uncertain whether Donna qualifies as a friend yet. Ilana immediately validates: 'Totally! Right?' — performing understanding. Both of them are slightly unsure what to call this new relationship.
jokes.explanation: A sweet small beat about how quickly 'Smelly Pussy Donna' became a genuine friend to Abbi. The hesitation before 'friend' is the laugh.

jokes.text: Then I realized, like, I need to put myself out there alone, to meet all these like, rad art contacts. / Right? / Dude, I met Toy Harris. / Whoa! Who is that, though?
jokes.setup: Abbi excitedly tells Ilana she met the incredible artist Toy Harris
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'Whoa! Who is that, though?' — enthusiasm first, question second — reveals she has no idea who Toy Harris is but is performing art-world engagement
jokes.explanation: The 'Whoa! Who is that, though?' structure is a perfect Ilana-ism — leading with the emotional response and trailing with the intellectual question. The implication is that Ilana would be equally impressed whether Toy Harris was the president or a street performer. Good character integration.

jokes.text: And then I realized the only reason I wasn't automatically asking them out was because she was a woman. / You don't say.
jokes.setup: Abbi describes her realization at the E.R. about the hot doctor
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'You don't say' — a perfectly delivered 'no shit' response — suggests this is information that is utterly unsurprising to Ilana, who has presumably known or suspected about Abbi's capacity to be attracted to women long before Abbi did
jokes.explanation: Earns its 8+ through character integration and economy. 'You don't say.' — three words — does enormous work here: it's Ilana's knowing acknowledgment of something she apparently already knew about Abbi, delivered with zero judgment and maximum 'obviously.' The joke works because it positions Ilana as someone who sees Abbi more clearly than Abbi sees herself, which is a core dynamic of the show. It's also the show's casual, matter-of-fact treatment of bisexuality/queer identity — no big deal, Ilana basically knew. Exceptional economy.

jokes.text: Yes! Cool! Cool! Cool! Cool! Cool! Cool! C-C-Cool! Cool. That is so... cool.
jokes.setup: Abbi reveals she asked the doctor out and they're going on a date Friday
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'Cool' repetition spiral — escalating from enthusiastic to a stutter to a landing on flat 'cool' — is a comedy performance of someone trying very hard to be chill about exciting news while clearly not being chill
jokes.explanation: A great performance joke. The 'cool' spiral is Ilana's attempt to not make a big deal of something (which would be anti-cool) while clearly exploding with excitement. The stutter ('C-C-Cool!') and the landing on flat 'cool. That is so... cool.' is structurally excellent — the deflation after the stutter is the punchline. Strong character integration: Ilana's relationship to 'cool' as a social value is a running character trait.
